  • Materials
  • Simulation
  • Estimation
  • Tools
marapendi
  • Models
  • View page source

Models

Mathematical and physical models for transport, electrochemistry, voltage, gas composition, water balance, transient dynamics, and degradation.

Transient cell model for PEM/AEM electrochemical cells.

Classes

TransientCellModelODE engine that wraps a Cell and a VoltageModel to

assemble and integrate the full spatially-resolved transient equations (water content, temperature, gas concentrations, liquid saturation).

class marapendi.models.transient.TransientCellModel(cell: ~marapendi.components.cell.Cell = <factory>)[source]

Bases: object

Spatially-resolved transient ODE model for a PEM/AEM cell.

The model assembles dxdt for the state vector x[n_layers, n_variables] where the variables are:

  • i_lmbd = 0 : ionomer water content λ

  • i_T    = 1 : temperature T [K]

  • i_cg   = [2,3,4,5] : gas concentrations [O₂, N₂, H₂, H₂O] [kmol m⁻³]

  • i_s    = 6 : liquid water saturation s [–]

Parameters:

  • cell (Cell) – Fully-configured cell geometry and property arrays.

  • charge (str) – Charge carrier ('proton' for PEM, 'hydroxide' for AEM).

cell: Cell
get_states_from_x(x)[source]

Unpack normalised state vector into named fields.

rates_of_change(x, i=0.0)[source]

Return dxdt for all state variables across all layers.

Parameters:

  • x (ndarray, shape (n_layers * n_variables, m))

  • i (float)

Module providing gas and liquid transport models.

class marapendi.models.transport_models.ChannelGasResistanceModel(sherwood: float = 4.0, B_ch: float = 1.0)[source]

Bases: object

Channel gas transport resistance model using Sherwood number approach. Similar equations are used by Kim et al. (2022).

sherwood

Sherwood number for mass transfer.

Type:

float

B_ch

Coefficient for convection resistance.

Type:

float

References

Kim, H. et al. Int. J. Heat Mass Transf. 183, 122106 (2022).

B_ch: float = 1.0
convection_resistance(channel, volume_flow_rate)[source]

Compute convection resistance with channel geometry. Assumes that the channel gas concentration is equal to the average between inlet and outlet values.

Parameters:

  • channel (FlowChannel) – Channel geometry.

  • volume_flow_rate (float) – Volumetric flow rate [m3/s].

Returns:

Channel convection resistance [s/m].

Return type:

float

molecular_diffusion_resistance(channel, diffusion_coefficient)[source]

Compute molecular diffusion resistance using Sherwood number.

Parameters:

  • channel (FlowChannel) – Channel geometry.

  • diffusion_coefficient (float) – Diffusion coefficient [m2/s].

Returns:

Channel diffusion resistance [s/m].

Return type:

float

sherwood: float = 4.0
total_resistance(channel, diffusion_coefficient, volume_flow_rate)[source]

Compute total resistance combining diffusion and convection.

Parameters:

  • channel (FlowChannel) – Channel geometry.

  • diffusion_coefficient (float) – Diffusion coefficient [m2/s].

  • volume_flow_rate (float) – Volumetric flow rate [m3/s].

Returns:

Channel total resistance [s/m].

Return type:

float

class marapendi.models.transport_models.DarcyTransportModel[source]

Bases: object

Model for calculating non-wetting phase transport in porous layers using a Darcy-based approach.

J_function_exponent

Exponent in the non-wetting phase capillary pressure-saturation relation.

Type:

float

calculate_liquid_darcy_flow_resistance(s, nu_l, thickness, K_abs, n_rel)[source]
capillary_pressure_from_saturation(s, p_b, m, n)[source]

Van Genuchten model

saturation_from_capillary_pressure(layer, p_c, p_b, m, n)[source]

Van Genuchten model

class marapendi.models.transport_models.MembraneWaterTransportModel[source]

Bases: object

‘ See Wei et al. (2023)

calculate_membrane_water_resistance(D_lmbd, thickness, eps_ion, c_ion, tort_ion)[source]
diffusion_coefficient(lmbd, f_v, T, darken_num, darken_den, alpha_lmbd, E_act, T_ref=303.15)[source]
equilibrium_water_content(rh, sorption_coeffs)[source]
liquid_equilibrium_water_content(reference_liquid_water_content)[source]
sorption_coefficient(f_v, T, k_des, E_act, T_ref=303.15)[source]
water_vol_fraction(lmbd, V_w, V_ion)[source]
class marapendi.models.transport_models.PorousGasResistanceModel(n_s: float = 3.0)[source]

Bases: object

Porous media gas transport model with water saturation and Knudsen corrections.

n_s

Exponent for empirical water saturation correction.

Type:

float

knudsen_diffusivity(T, d_p, M_k)[source]
molecular_diffusion_effective_length(thickness, eps_p, tort, s, n_s)[source]
molecular_diffusion_resistance(D_g_k, thickness, eps_p, tort, s, n_s)[source]
n_s: float = 3.0
total_diffusion_resistance(T, s, D_g_k, M_k, thickness, eps_p, tort, d_p, n_s)[source]

Compute total diffusion resistance (molecular + Knudsen).

Returns:

Total resistance [s/m].

Return type:

float

water_saturation_correction(s, n_s)[source]

Compute correction factor for effective diffusivity.

Parameters:

water_saturation (float) – Water saturation [-].

Returns:

Correction factor [-].

Return type:

float

Electrochemical voltage model for PEM/AEM fuel cells.

Classes

VoltageModelComputes reversible voltage, overpotentials, and cell voltage.

Stateless strategy object — all inputs are passed explicitly so methods can be tested and reused independently of the ODE machinery.

class marapendi.models.voltage_models.VoltageModel[source]

Bases: object

Electrochemical voltage model for a single PEM/AEM cell.

calculate_activation_overpotential(T_ca_cl, p_o2_ca_cl, i, i_x, theta_PtO, ca_cl)[source]

Net activation overpotential including Pt-oxide voltage drop.

calculate_cell_voltage(T_an_cl, T_ca_cl, T_memb, f_v_memb, f_v_ca_cl, p_h2, p_o2_local, p_o2_ca_cl, i, memb_thickness, electrical_resistance, memb, ca_cl, charge, theta_PtO=0)[source]

Cell voltage and per-location overpotential components.

The caller assembles S_T_losses from the returned components.

Return type:

V_cell, eta_ohm, eta_act, E_rev_ca, E_rev_an, eta_memb, eta_ca_cl, eta_gdl

calculate_h2_permeation_flux(T_memb, f_v_memb, p_h2, memb_thickness, memb)[source]

H2 crossover flux through the membrane [kmol m⁻² s⁻¹].

calculate_ohmic_overpotential(T_memb, f_v_memb, T_ca_cl, f_v_ca_cl, i, memb_thickness, electrical_resistance, memb, ca_cl, charge)[source]

Per-location ohmic overpotential.

Returns:

eta_memb, eta_ca_cl, eta_gdl

Return type:

ndarray

calculate_orr_overpotential(T_ca_cl, p_o2_ca_cl, i, i_x, roughness_factor, reaction)[source]

ORR Tafel overpotential at the cathode catalyst layer.

calculate_reversible_cell_voltage(T_an_cl, T_ca_cl, p_h2, p_o2_local)[source]

Thermodynamic reversible cell voltage and half-cell potentials.

Returns:

E_rev, E_rev_ca, E_rev_an

Return type:

ndarray

Module providing electrochemistry functions.

class marapendi.models.electrochemistry.ElectrochemicalReaction(reference_exchange_current_density: float = 0.0741, activation_energy: float = 67000000.0, reaction_order: float = 0.54, reference_activity: float = 100000.0, reference_temperature: float = 353.15, number_of_electrons: int = 2, charge_transfer_coeff: float = 0.5)[source]

Bases: object

A dataclass representing the parameters of an electrochemical reaction.

Attributes:

reference_exchange_current_densityfloat

The reference exchange current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

activation_energyfloat, optional, default=0.0741

The activation energy of the reaction in Joules (J/kmol).

reaction_orderfloat, optional, default=1.0

The reaction order with respect to the reactant activity (dimensionless).

reference_activityfloat, optional, default=1.0

The reference reactant activity (dimensionless). Typically set to 1 for normalized activities or standard concentrations.

reference_temperaturefloat, optional, default=300

The reference temperature in Kelvin (K), for the reference exchange current density.

number_of_electronsint, optional, default=2

Number of electrons transferred in the electrochemical reaction.

charge_transfer_coefffloat, optional, default=0.5

Symmetry factor or charge transfer coefficient (dimensionless).

Methods:

exchange_current_density(temperature, reactant_activity):

Calculate the exchange current density for the reaction under specific conditions.

tafel_overpotential(current_density, temperature, reactant_activity):

Calculate the Tafel overpotential for the reaction under specific conditions.

Example:

>>> reaction = ElectrochemicalReaction(
...     reference_exchange_current_density=1e-4,
...     activation_energy=50e6,
...     number_of_electrons=2,
...     charge_transfer_coeff=0.5
... )
activation_energy: float = 67000000.0
charge_transfer_coeff: float = 0.5
exchange_current_density(temperature, reactant_activity)[source]

Calculate the exchange current density for the electrochemical reaction.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

reactant_activityfloat

Activity of the reactant (dimensionless), typically a ratio or concentration.

Returns:

float

The exchange current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

Notes:

This method uses the calculate_exchange_current_density function and passes the dataclass instance (self) to encapsulate the reaction parameters.

Example:

>>> reaction = ElectrochemicalReaction(
    reference_exchange_current_density=1e-4,
    activation_energy=50e6
)
>>> i0 = reaction.exchange_current_density(temperature=310, reactant_activity=0.5)
linear_overpotential(current_density, temperature, reactant_activity)[source]

Calculate the linear overpotential for the electrochemical reaction.

Parameters:

current_densityfloat

The operating current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

reactant_activityfloat

Activity of the reactant (dimensionless), typically a ratio or concentration.

Returns:

float

The linear overpotential in Volts (V).

Example:

>>> reaction = ElectrochemicalReaction(
...     reference_exchange_current_density=1e-4,
...     activation_energy=50e6,
...     number_of_electrons=2,
...     charge_transfer_coeff=0.5
... )
>>> eta = reaction.linear_overpotential(
    current_density=1e-3, temperature=310, reactant_activity=0.5
)
number_of_electrons: int = 2
reaction_order: float = 0.54
reference_activity: float = 100000.0
reference_exchange_current_density: float = 0.0741
reference_temperature: float = 353.15
tafel_overpotential(current_density, temperature, reactant_activity)[source]

Calculate the Tafel overpotential for the electrochemical reaction.

Parameters:

current_densityfloat

The operating current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

reactant_activityfloat

Activity of the reactant (dimensionless), typically a ratio or concentration.

Returns:

float

The Tafel overpotential in Volts (V).

Example:

>>> reaction = ElectrochemicalReaction(
...     reference_exchange_current_density=1e-4,
...     activation_energy=50e6,
...     number_of_electrons=2,
...     charge_transfer_coeff=0.5
... )
>>> eta = reaction.tafel_overpotential(
    current_density=1e-3, temperature=310, reactant_activity=0.5
)
tafel_slope(temperature)[source]

Calculate the Tafel slope for the electrochemical reaction.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

Returns:

float

The Tafel slope in Volts (V/decade).

marapendi.models.electrochemistry.calculate_exchange_current_density(temperature: float, reactant_activity: float, params: ElectrochemicalReaction)[source]

Calculate the exchange current density for an electrochemical reaction with a single reactant.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

reactant_activityfloat

Activity of the reactant (dimensionless), typically a ratio or concentration.

paramsElectrochemicalReaction

An instance of the ElectrochemicalReaction dataclass containing the reaction parameters: - reference_exchange_current_density : float

The reference exchange current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

  • activation_energyfloat

    Activation energy in Joules (J).

  • reaction_orderfloat

    Reaction order with respect to the reactant activity (dimensionless).

  • reference_activityfloat

    Reference reactant activity (dimensionless). Defaults to 1.0.

  • reference_temperaturefloat

    Reference temperature in Kelvin (K). Defaults to 300 K.

Returns:

float

The exchange current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

marapendi.models.electrochemistry.calculate_linear_overpotential(current_density, exchange_current_density, temperature, number_of_electrons, charge_transfer_coeff)[source]

Calculate the linear overpotential for an electrochemical reaction. This is an approximation of the Butler-Volmer is valid for low overpotentials (< 0.1 V).

Parameters:

current_densityfloat

The operating current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

exchange_current_densityfloat

The exchange current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

number_of_electronsint

Number of electrons transferred in the electrochemical reaction.

charge_transfer_coefficientfloat

Symmetry factor or charge transfer coefficient (dimensionless).

Returns:

float

The Tafel overpotential in Volts (V).

Example:

>>> calculate_tafel_overpotential(
...     current_density=1e4,
...     exchange_current_density=1e-3,
...     temperature=298.15,
...     number_of_electrons=2,
...     charge_transfer_coeff=0.5
... )
0.17985
marapendi.models.electrochemistry.calculate_reversible_cell_voltage(temperature, activities_ratio)[source]

Calculate the reversible cell voltage of a hydrogen fuel cell or electrolyser, using Nernst equation.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat

Temperature of the cell in Kelvin (K).

partial_pressure_o2float

Partial pressure of oxygen (O₂) in Pascals (Pa).

partial_pressure_h2float

Partial pressure of hydrogen (H₂) in Pascals (Pa).

Returns:

float

Reversible cell voltage in Volts (V).

Notes:

The function computes the reversible cell voltage using the thermodynamic relationship:

E_rev = (-ΔG° + ΔS°(T - T°) + RT ln(Q)) / (2F)

where Q is the reaction quotient based on the partial pressures of H₂ and O₂.

Constants:

  • R: Universal gas constant, 8.314 J/(mol·K).

  • F: Faraday constant, 96485 C/mol.

Example:

>>> reversible_cell_voltage(T=300, p_o2=2e5, p_h2=1e5)
1.1901
marapendi.models.electrochemistry.calculate_tafel_overpotential(current_density, exchange_current_density, temperature, number_of_electrons, charge_transfer_coeff)[source]

Calculate the Tafel overpotential for an electrochemical reaction. This is an approximation of the Butler-Volmer is valid for high overpotentials (> 0.1 V).

Parameters:

current_densityfloat

The operating current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

exchange_current_densityfloat

The exchange current density in Amperes per square meter (A/m²).

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

number_of_electronsint

Number of electrons transferred in the electrochemical reaction.

charge_transfer_coefficientfloat

Symmetry factor or charge transfer coefficient (dimensionless).

Returns:

float

The Tafel overpotential in Volts (V).

Example:

>>> calculate_tafel_overpotential(
...     current_density=1e4,
...     exchange_current_density=1e-3,
...     temperature=298.15,
...     number_of_electrons=2,
...     charge_transfer_coeff=0.5
... )
0.17985
marapendi.models.electrochemistry.calculate_tafel_slope(temperature, number_of_electrons, charge_transfer_coeff)[source]

Calculate the Tafel slope for an electrochemical reaction.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

number_of_electronsint

Number of electrons transferred in the electrochemical reaction.

charge_transfer_coefficientfloat

Symmetry factor or charge transfer coefficient (dimensionless).

Returns:

float

The Tafel slope in Volts (V/decade).

Module providing a class to handle gas composition and properties.

class marapendi.models.gas_composition.GasComposition(temperature=300, pressure=100000.0)[source]

Bases: object

A class for managing and calculating gas compositions, including temperature, pressure, and relative humidity effects on the gas phase.

The gas is composed of O2, N2, H2 and H2O.

Attributes:

gasct.Solution

Cantera Solution object for the gas mixture with selected species.

relative_humidityfloat

The relative humidity of the gas mixture.

saturation_pressurefloat

The saturation pressure of water at the current temperature in Pascals (Pa).

Methods:

set_pressure(new_pressure):

Set the pressure of the gas mixture and update the relative humidity.

set_temperature(new_temperature):

Set the temperature of the gas mixture, updating the saturation pressure and relative humidity.

set_temperature_and_pressure(temperature, pressure):

Set both the temperature and pressure of the gas mixture.

pressure():

Get the current pressure of the gas mixture in Pascals (Pa).

temperature():

Get the current temperature of the gas mixture in Kelvin (K).

set_composition(dry_o2_mole_fraction, dry_h2_mole_fraction, relative_humidity):

Set the composition of the gas mixture, including dry gas oxygen and hydrogen mole fractions, and relative humidity.

__init__(temperature=300, pressure=100000.0)[source]

Initialize the gas composition with a given temperature and pressure.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat, optional, default=300

Temperature of the gas mixture in Kelvin (K).

pressurefloat, optional, default=1e5

Pressure of the gas mixture in Pascals (Pa).

calculate_mixture_kinematic_viscosity()[source]
calculate_relative_humidity() → float[source]

Calculate the current relative humidity in the gas mixture.

Returns:

float

Relative humidity between 0 and 1.

calculate_species_diffusion_coefficient(species)[source]

Calculate the binary diffusion coefficient for a given species in the gas phase.

Uses empirical correlations based on reference values adjusted for temperature and pressure. Data from Vetter and Schumacher (2019).

Parameters:

species (str) – The chemical species (‘o2’, ‘h2’, or ‘h2o’) for which to compute the diffusion coefficient.

Returns:

  • float – The adjusted diffusion coefficient [m^2/s].

  • Reference

  • ———-

  • Vetter, R. & Schumacher, J. O. Comput. Phys. Commun. 234, 223–234 (2019).

calculate_species_kinematic_viscosity(species)[source]
concentration()[source]
density()[source]
get_relative_humidity() → float[source]

Get the current relative humidity in the gas mixture.

Returns:

float

Relative humidity between 0 and 1.

pressure() → float[source]

Get the current pressure of the gas mixture.

Returns:

float

Pressure in Pascals (Pa).

set_composition(dry_o2_mole_fraction: float, dry_h2_mole_fraction: float, relative_humidity: float)[source]

Set the composition of the gas mixture.

The mole fraction of water vapor is calculated based on the relative humidity and the saturation pressure at the current temperature. The wet gas composition is updated accordingly.

Parameters:

dry_o2_mole_fractionfloat

Mole fraction of oxygen in the dry gas mixture.

dry_h2_mole_fractionfloat

Mole fraction of hydrogen in the dry gas mixture.

relative_humidityfloat

Relative humidity of the gas mixture.

set_pressure(new_pressure: float)[source]

Set the pressure of the gas mixture and adjust the relative humidity.

Parameters:

new_pressurefloat

The new pressure in Pascals (Pa).

set_temperature(new_temperature: float)[source]

Set the temperature of the gas mixture and adjust the relative humidity and kinematic viscosity.

Parameters:

new_temperaturefloat

The new temperature in Kelvin (K).

set_temperature_and_pressure(temperature: float, pressure: float)[source]

Set both the temperature and pressure of the gas mixture.

Parameters:

temperaturefloat

The temperature in Kelvin (K).

pressurefloat

The pressure in Pascals (Pa).

temperature() → float[source]

Get the current temperature of the gas mixture.

Returns:

float

Temperature in Kelvin (K).

vapor_pressure() → float[source]

Get the current vapor partial pressure in the gas mixture.

Returns:

float

Vapor partial pressure in Pa.

marapendi.models.gas_composition.calculate_species_diffusion_coefficient(temperature, pressure, x_h2=0)[source]

Calculate the binary diffusion coefficient for a given species in the gas phase.

Uses empirical correlations based on reference values adjusted for temperature and pressure. Data from Vetter and Schumacher (2019).

Returns:

  • float – The adjusted diffusion coefficient [m^2/s].

  • Reference

  • ———-

  • Vetter, R. & Schumacher, J. O. Comput. Phys. Commun. 234, 223–234 (2019).

class marapendi.models.water_balance_models.MembraneWaterBalanceModel(sorption_activity_driving_force: bool = False, eod_parallel_to_sorption: bool = False)[source]

Bases: object

A class representing a membrane water balance model with various parameters related to water diffusivity and absorption.

sorption_activity_driving_force

Boolean flag indicating whether water activity is the driving force for water absorption (default is False). If false, water content difference is considered as the driving force.

Type:

bool, optional

eod_parallel_to_sorption

Boolean flag indicating if electro-osmotic drag is parallel to water absorption/desorption (default is False). If True, electro-osmotic drag is added to the water absorption flux on the RHS of the water balance boundary conditions.

Type:

bool, optional

Notes

The class is based on the equations and assumptions in Ferrara et al. (2018), while accounting for gas transport resistance and non-equilibrium conditions at the membrane interface.

References:

Ferrara, A. et al. J. Power Sources 390, 197–207 (2018).

calculate_anode_membrane_flux(cell)[source]

Calculate the water flux at the anode/membrane interface.

Parameters:

cell (FuelCell object) – An object representing the cell with properties related to water content and transport.

Returns:

The calculated water flux at the anode (kmol/m²/s).

Return type:

float

calculate_biot_number(absorption_coefficient, membrane, water_diffusivity)[source]

Calculate the Biot number using absorption coefficient, membrane thickness, and water diffusivity.

Parameters:

  • absorption_coefficient (float) – The water absorption coefficient (m/s).

  • membrane (object) – An object with membrane properties, including dry thickness.

  • water_diffusivity (float) – The membrane water diffusivity (m²/s).

Returns:

The calculated Biot number, dimensionless.

Return type:

float

calculate_cathode_membrane_flux(cell)[source]

Calculate the water flux at the cathode/membrane interface.

Parameters:

cell (FuelCell object) – An object representing the cell with properties related to water content and transport.

Returns:

The calculated water flux at the cathode (kmol/m²/s).

Return type:

float

calculate_peclet_number(temperature, current_density, membrane, water_diffusivity)[source]

Calculate the Peclet number using electroosmotic drag speed, membrane thickness, and water diffusivity.

Parameters:

  • temperature (float) – The temperature at which to calculate the Peclet number, in Kelvin (K).

  • current_density (float) – The current density crossing the membrane (A/m2).

  • membrane (object) – An object with membrane properties, including dry thickness (m).

  • water_diffusivity (float) – The adsorbed water diffusivity (m²/s).

Returns:

The calculated Peclet number, dimensionless.

Return type:

float

eod_parallel_to_sorption: bool = False
estimate_equilibrium_water_contents(cell)[source]

Estimates the equilibrium water content and its derivative for both the cathode and anode sides of the cell.

Parameters:

cell (FuelCell) – An object representing the cell with properties ca (cathode) and an (anode), each being a CellSide object.

Notes

The function updates the cell sides (ca and an) with estimated water content and its derivative. The calculations assume no water crossover from cathode to anode for RH at catalyst layer.

solve_water_balance(cell, water_profile=None, dynamic=False)[source]

Calculate and update the water balance properties in the cell.

Parameters:

  • cell (FuelCell) – Cell object containing membrane, cathode (ca), and anode (an) components.

  • water_profile (np.ndarray, optional) – Initial water content profile, required when dynamic=True.

  • dynamic (bool) – If True, solves for a prescribed water profile without updating fluxes.

sorption_activity_driving_force: bool = False
update_cell_side_water_fluxes(cell_side, dynamic)[source]

Calculate the liquid and vapor fluxes for a given side of the cell (cathode or anode).

Parameters:

cell_side (CellSide object) – An object representing a side of the cell with properties related to water and vapor content, transport, and other physical properties.

Notes

This function calculates the maximum vapor removal flux and then determines the liquid flux by comparing the water flux with the maximum vapor removal flux.

update_non_dimensional_parameters(cell)[source]

Calculate various non-dimensional parameters related to water transport and equilibrium in a cell.

Parameters:

cell (FuelCell object) – An object representing the cell with properties related to water content, transport, membrane properties, and other physical and structural properties.

Notes

This function computes various non-dimensional parameters used in the analysis of water balance within the cell, including the Peclet number, non-dimensional water vapor resistance, Biot number, and others.

update_water_contents(cell)[source]

Set the water content values for different parts of the cell, including catalyst layers and membrane.

Parameters:

cell (FuelCell object) – An object representing the cell with properties ca (cathode), an (anode), membrane, etc., each having attributes related to water content and transport properties.

Notes

This function updates the following attributes of the cell: - Equilibrium water content in the cathode and anode catalyst layers. - Average water content in the membrane. - Ionomer water content in the cathode and anode catalyst layers.

update_water_fluxes(cell, dynamic=False)[source]

Calculate water fluxes for both the cathode and anode sides of the cell.

Parameters:

cell (object) – An object representing the cell with properties related to water content, transport, and other physical properties for cathode and anode.

Notes

This function calculates the water flux for both the cathode and anode sides of the cell, and then calculates the liquid flux for each side.

update_water_profile(cell)[source]

Calculate the water content profile across the cell based on various parameters.

Parameters:

cell (FuelCell object) – An object representing the cell with properties related to water content, transport, membrane properties, and other physical and structural properties.

Notes

This function computes the water content profile based on an extension of the work of Ferrara et al. (2018) accounting for non-equilibrium conditions at the membrane interfaces. Parameters are calculated in the water_balance functions.

Module providing classes describing platinum degradation mechanisms in electrochemical cells (e.g. PEM fuel cells).

The implementation follows kinetic formulations proposed in:

Darling & Meyers, “Kinetic Model of Platinum Dissolution in PEMFCs”, Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 150(11), A1523 (2003).

The module includes:

  • Platinum particle size distributions

  • Material thermodynamic properties

  • Platinum dissolution kinetics

  • Platinum oxide formation/dissolution

  • Coupled degradation rate evaluation

class marapendi.models.degradation.CarbonCorrosion(rate_constant: float = 1.5e-22, potential_dependency: float = 19, reference_potential: float = 0.2)[source]

Bases: object

potential_dependency: float = 19
rate_constant: float = 1.5e-22
reaction_rate(potential)[source]
reference_potential: float = 0.2
class marapendi.models.degradation.CathodicDissolution(rate_constant: float = 1.4e-08)[source]

Bases: object

Cathodic platinum dissolution kinetics.

Model based on: Schneider et al. (2019), J. Electrochem. Soc.

rate_constant: float = 1.4e-08
rate_of_reaction(place_exchanged_oxide_coverage, limiting_place_exchanged_coverage)[source]
class marapendi.models.degradation.OxidePlaceExchange(forward_rate_constant: float = 1e-21, backward_rate_constant: float = 1e-19, reference_potential: float = 1.2, transfer_coeff: float = 0.5, omega_forward: float = 10000000.0, omega_backward: float = 90000000.0)[source]

Bases: object

Oxide place exchange reaction kinetics.

Model based on: Schneider et al. (2019), J. Electrochem. Soc.

backward_rate_constant: float = 1e-19
forward_rate(potential, platinum_oxide_coverage, temperature)[source]
forward_rate_constant: float = 1e-21
limiting_coverage(potential, platinum_oxide_coverage, temperature)[source]
omega_backward: float = 90000000.0
omega_forward: float = 10000000.0
rate_of_reaction(platinum_oxide_coverage, place_exchanged_oxide_coverage, potential, temperature)[source]

Net place-exchanged oxide formation rate [kmol/m²/s].

reference_potential: float = 1.2
transfer_coeff: float = 0.5
class marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumDissolution(rate_constant: float = 3.43e-23, reference_potential: float = 1.188, transfer_coeff_ca: float = 0.5, transfer_coeff_an: float = 0.5, relative_humidity_power: float = 1.7, dissolved_platinum_reference_concentration: float = 1.0)[source]

Bases: object

Platinum electrochemical dissolution reaction.

Reference:

Schneider et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 2019, 166 (4), F322–F333.

dissolved_platinum_reference_concentration: float = 1.0
equilibrium_potential(particle_radius)[source]

Size-dependent equilibrium potential including Gibbs–Thomson correction.

rate_constant: float = 3.43e-23
rate_of_reaction(dissolved_platinum_concentration, platinum_oxide_coverage, potential, temperature, relative_humidity, particle_radius)[source]

Net platinum dissolution rate [kmol/m²/s].

reference_potential: float = 1.188
relative_humidity_power: float = 1.7
transfer_coeff_an: float = 0.5
transfer_coeff_ca: float = 0.5
class marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumOxideDissolution(rate_constant: float = 3.2e-23)[source]

Bases: object

Chemical dissolution of platinum oxide.

Reaction couples oxide reduction and platinum ion concentration in the ionomer phase.

Based on Darling & Meyers (2003).

rate_constant: float = 3.2e-23
rate_of_reaction(dissolved_platinum_concentration, platinum_oxide_coverage, proton_concentration, temperature, particle_radius, platinum_dissolution, platinum_oxide_formation)[source]

Oxide dissolution reaction rate [kmol/m²/s].

class marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumOxideFormation(rate_constant: float = 1.36e-10, reference_potential: float = 0.98, transfer_coeff_ca: float = 0.5, transfer_coeff_an: float = 0.35, omega_platinum_oxide_formation: float = 30000000.0, proton_reference_concentration: float = 1.0)[source]

Bases: object

Platinum oxide formation reaction kinetics.

Model based on: Darling & Meyers (2003), J. Electrochem. Soc.

Describes electrochemical oxidation of metallic platinum:

Pt + H2O ⇌ PtO + 2H+ + 2e-

Parameters:

  • rate_constant (float [kmol/m²/s]) – Reaction kinetic prefactor.

  • reference_potential (float [V]) – Standard equilibrium potential.

  • transfer_coeff_ca (float) – Cathodic transfer coefficient.

  • transfer_coeff_an (float) – Anodic transfer coefficient.

  • omega_platinum_oxide_formation (float [J/kmol]) – Lateral interaction parameter between oxide species.

  • proton_reference_concentration (float [kmol/m³]) – Reference proton concentration.

equilibrium_potential(particle_radius)[source]

Size-dependent oxide formation equilibrium potential.

Includes Gibbs–Thomson curvature correction between platinum and platinum oxide phases.

omega_platinum_oxide_formation: float = 30000000.0
proton_reference_concentration: float = 1.0
rate_constant: float = 1.36e-10
rate_of_reaction(platinum_oxide_coverage, proton_concentration, potential, temperature, particle_radius)[source]

Net platinum oxide formation rate [kmol/m²/s].

reference_potential: float = 0.98
transfer_coeff_an: float = 0.35
transfer_coeff_ca: float = 0.5
class marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumSpecies(density: float, molecular_weight: float, surface_tension: float, reference_pontential: float = 0)[source]

Bases: object

Thermodynamic properties of a platinum phase.

Parameters:

  • density (float [kg/m³])

  • molecular_weight (float [kg/kmol])

  • surface_tension (float [J/m²])

  • reference_pontial (float [J/kmol]) – Reference Gibbs potential.

__post_init__()[source]

Compute molar volume.

density: float
molecular_weight: float
reference_pontential: float = 0
surface_tension: float
surface_tension_potential_shift(particle_radius)[source]

Gibbs–Thomson potential correction due to nanoparticle curvature.

class marapendi.models.degradation.PtDissolution(platinum_dissolution: ~marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumDissolution = <factory>, platinum_oxide_formation: ~marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumOxideFormation = <factory>, platinum_oxide_dissolution: ~marapendi.models.degradation.PlatinumOxideDissolution = <factory>, platinum_cathodic_dissolution: ~marapendi.models.degradation.CathodicDissolution = <factory>, platinum_oxide_place_exchange: ~marapendi.models.degradation.OxidePlaceExchange = <factory>, carbon_corrosion: ~marapendi.models.degradation.CarbonCorrosion = <factory>, catalyst_layer: ~marapendi.components.catalyst_layers.CatalystLayer = <factory>)[source]

Bases: object

Coupled platinum degradation model.

Combines:

  • metallic platinum dissolution

  • oxide formation

  • oxide dissolution

The model evolves:

  • particle radius distribution

  • dissolved platinum concentration

using surface-integrated reaction rates.

carbon_corrosion: CarbonCorrosion
catalyst_layer: CatalystLayer
platinum_cathodic_dissolution: CathodicDissolution
platinum_dissolution: PlatinumDissolution
platinum_oxide_dissolution: PlatinumOxideDissolution
platinum_oxide_formation: PlatinumOxideFormation
platinum_oxide_place_exchange: OxidePlaceExchange
time_derivatives(dissolved_platinum_concentration, platinum_oxide_coverage, place_exchanged_oxide_coverage, proton_concentration, potential, temperature, relative_humidity, particle_radius, particle_number_density)[source]

Compute degradation time derivatives.

Returns:

  • particle_radius_time_derivative (ndarray [m/s]) – Radius evolution for each particle size bin.

  • dissolved_platinum_concentration_time_derivative – float [kmol/m³/s] Dissolved platinum accumulation rate.

  • Reference

  • ———-

  • Schneider et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 2019, 166 (4), F322–F333.

  • Darling; Meyers. J. Electrochem. Soc. 2003, 150 (11), A1523.

class marapendi.models.degradation.PtSizeDistribution(number_density_array: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]], r_array: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]], n_points: int = 32, r_mean: float = 0, r_std: float = 1e-09, initial_platinum_loading: float = 0.002, initial_cl_thickness: float = 1e-05, initial_ecsa: float = 40000.0, distribution_type: str = 'norm')[source]

Bases: object

Represents a platinum nanoparticle size distribution inside the catalyst layer.

The distribution is discretized into radius bins used for degradation calculations.

Parameters:

  • n_points (int) – Number of discretization points.

  • r_mean (float [m]) – Mean particle radius.

  • r_std (float [m]) – Standard deviation of particle radius.

  • initial_platinum_loading (float [kg/m²]) – Platinum loading.

  • initial_cl_thickness (float [m]) – Catalyst layer thickness.

  • initial_ecsa (float [m²/kg]) – Initial electrochemical surface area.

  • distribution_type ({"norm","lognorm"}) – Type of statistical radius distribution.

__post_init__()[source]

Initialize statistical distribution and derived quantities.

average_particle_mass(normalized=False)[source]

Return average particle mass [kg].

average_particle_surface(normalized=False)[source]

Return average particle surface area [m²].

average_particle_volume(normalized=False)[source]

Return average particle volume [m³].

distribution_type: str = 'norm'
ecsa()[source]

Electrochemical surface area accounting for utilization losses.

geometrical_specific_surface_area()[source]

Geometrical surface per unit mass [m²/kg].

initial_cl_thickness: float = 1e-05
initial_ecsa: float = 40000.0
initial_number_density()[source]

Particle number density inside catalyst layer [1/m³].

initial_platinum_loading: float = 0.002
n_points: int = 32
number_density()[source]
number_density_array: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]
platinum_loading()[source]
plot_distribution(ax=None, label='Pt radius distribution')[source]

Plot particle radius probability density.

r_array: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]
r_mean: float = 0
r_std: float = 1e-09
